coachNotes: The rook slides to the 8th rank. Black's king is trapped by its own pawns on f7, g7, h7.
commonErrors: Rxb2 — captures nothing useful

alternatives.reason: Loses the rook for no compensation

coachNotes: The queen captures f7 with protection from the c4 bishop. This is the famous Scholar's Mate pattern.
The knight delivers checkmate because the king is surrounded by its own pieces (g8 rook, g7 and h7 pawns).
Actually it is Black who already delivered Qh4# (Fool's Mate). This teaches why f3+g4 is terrible.
The white king supports the rook on the 8th rank. Simple but demonstrates the K+R vs K mating technique.
White's rook invades on the a-file. After Rxa8 Rxa8, the remaining rook delivers back rank mate.
The queen occupies e8 with no way for the black king to escape. The f7 pawn blocks f7 escape.
The rook delivers check on d8, and the black king is boxed in by its own pawns on a7, b7, c7.
The bishop on c4 delivers check on f7. The king cannot escape because of piece congestion.
The rook controls the a-file while the king covers b6 and b7. The black king has no escape.
The completed Scholar's Mate. White's queen on f7 is protected by the bishop on c4.
The rook slides to e8 delivering checkmate. Black's king is trapped by its own g7, h7, f7 pawns.
Exchange on d8, then the remaining piece delivers back rank mate.
The rook delivers mate on the a-file with the king supporting from b2.
Qf8+ is checkmate because the knight on e7 blocks the escape route, and the rook is exchanged.
Rb8+ forces Rc8 (only move), then Rxc8# is back rank mate. The key is seeing that the rook deflects the defender.
The classic Greek Gift sacrifice! Bxh7+ destroys the kingside pawn shield, and Ng5+ sets up a discovered attack on the exposed king.
The queen sacrifice on e8 deflects the rook, then Nf7# is a beautiful smothered mate. The g8 rook, g7 pawn, and h7 pawn trap the king.
Another smothered mate pattern. The queen sacrifice on g8 forces Rxg8, and Nf7# is checkmate with the knight being untouchable.
The queen moves to a3, threatening Ra8#. Black must deal with the back rank threat but cannot cover everything.
Bxf7+ forces the king to an exposed square, then Qd5+ forks king and rook while threatening mate.
Qh3 targets h7 with mate threats. Black's defensive options are limited.
The rook exchange on c8 is followed by a devastating bishop move.
The knight jumps to f6, simultaneously attacking the king on d7 and the rook on e1. After the king moves, Nxe1 wins the rook.
Ng5 attacks the weak f7 square with both the knight and bishop. This is the beginning of the Fried Liver Attack.
Qd7 attacks both the c8 rook and the b4 bishop simultaneously. Black cannot defend both pieces.
Nxf7 forks the queen on d6 and the rook on h8. The knight is temporarily sacrificed but wins material.
Qa4 attacks the undefended b4 bishop and the c6 knight. Black loses material.
Nc7+ attacks the king on b7 and the a8 corner, demonstrating the knight's unique L-shaped fork.
Nxe5 attacks both the d7 bishop and threatens discovered attacks on the f7 pawn.
Bg5 pins the f6 knight to the d8 queen. The knight cannot move without exposing the queen to capture.
The bishop on b5 pins the c6 knight to the king. This is the Ruy Lopez main idea.
The pinned knight on f6 cannot recapture because it is pinned by Bg5 to the queen on d8. Bxf6 wins a piece.
Be2 unpins the knight while maintaining a solid position. The bishop on g4 loses its pin.
d5 attacks the pinned knight on c6. The knight cannot move because the queen on e7 is behind it.
Rh6+ skewers the king on c6 and the rook on a3. After the king moves, Rxa3 wins the rook.
The bishop on e5 checks the king on c6. After the king moves, Bxa1 wins the queen.
Re8+ forces the king to move, then Rxb1 captures the queen. A classic X-ray attack pattern.
Nxd5 uncovers the e2 bishop's attack on the g4 bishop. Black loses a pawn or the bishop pair.
Bb4+ is a discovered check setup in the Nimzo-Indian Defense. It pins the knight to the king.
Rb8+ deflects a potential defender. If the rook defends, the back rank is exposed.
Bxe6 deflects the f7 pawn from guarding critical squares. If fxe6, the e-file opens for the queen.
Qb8+ attracts the king to an inferior square, allowing a follow-up tactic.
The classic Greek Gift! Bxh7+ rips open the kingside. After Kxh7, Ng5+ continues the attack with Qh5 to follow. This pattern occurs frequently in the French Defense and Queen's Gambit Declined.
Bxc6+ trades bishop for knight but doubles Black's pawns. This is the Ruy Lopez Exchange variation.
Another Greek Gift pattern. The bishop sac opens the h-file and creates a mating attack with Ng5+ and Qh5.
White must take the opposition with Kd3! This ensures the pawn can promote. Ke3 also works but Kd3 is the most direct path to gaining the opposition.
The king must step aside to let the pawn promote. Ka7 prepares b8=Q next move.
Ra4+ pushes the black king away from the pawn, allowing White to advance it safely.
Kg6 takes the opposition. The king occupies a key square in front of the pawn, guaranteeing promotion.
White uses triangulation: Kc4-Kd4-Kd3 to lose a tempo and force Black into zugzwang.
An intermediate move before recapturing. Taking the d4 pawn first creates tactical opportunities before responding to threats.
d5 is an in-between move that attacks the knight before it can recapture. This gains tempo and space.
The f6 knight is overloaded — it defends both h7 and d5. Bxh7+ exploits this by attacking the square the knight must guard.
d5 attacks the overloaded c6 knight that defends both e7 and a5. Something must give.
Nh4 targets the light-squared bishop. In the Slav Defense, trading the light-squared bishop often favors White positionally.
cxd5 creates an isolated d-pawn for Black (after exd5). White plans to blockade d4 and exploit the weak d5 pawn.
This requires 3-move calculation. dxc5 wins a pawn, and after the recaptures, Bxg6 exploits the weakened kingside.
The capture sequence cxd5 Nxd5 Nxd5 is a forcing line. Each capture is essentially forced, simplifying the position in White's favor.
The two knights coordinate to deliver a mating attack. The knight on d3 blocks the king escape while the queen invades.
commonErrors: Nxd1 — captures material but misses the faster forced mate

alternatives.reason: Wins the exchange but gives White time to defend

coachNotes: The queen sacrifice clears the way for the bishop to deliver checkmate with rook support on the open g-file.
commonErrors: Bxf7+ first loses the tactical punch — Black can interpose

coachNotes: The knight reroutes via d5 to f6 creating a decisive discovered attack threat.
The bishop on g7 creates a mating net. Qh4 followed by Qxh6+ forces checkmate.
The knight discovers check on the king while simultaneously attacking the queen.
The knight maneuvers to deliver a smothered mate. The rook on g8 blocks the kings escape.
The knight zigzags between f7 and h6 creating perpetual check.
The king is hunted through the open center.
Nd5 prepares Ne7+ with a discovered attack on d7.
The queen and bishop form a deadly battery along the long diagonal.
A relentless king chase beginning with Qxf7+.
Bh6 tears apart the kingside pawn shield.
The knight and queen work together to cut off escape routes.
Nf6+ forces gxf6 opening the g-file.
Nd5 is a spectacular sacrifice.
Bb5 pins the knight to the king while pressing d5 indirectly.
Nxf7 attacks both queen and rook.
Nxe4 wins the knight and opens a discovery.
d5 attacks both knight c6 and bishop e6.
Nxd5 discovers an attack on the queen.
Nf6+ discovers check from bishop c4 while attacking the queen.
Bxf6 eliminates a defender while discovering pressure.
Clean discovered check from the c4 bishop.
Black must move the king since the bishop gives check.
The rook X-rays through the king to win the rook behind.
Qg8+ X-rays through to the rook on a8.
The bishop X-rays to defend while cutting off the king.
Pure back rank mate. The rook invades the 8th rank.
The queen delivers back rank mate when the rook is forced to move.
Exchange rooks on d8, then deliver back rank mate.
Rook lifts to e8 delivering checkmate.
Qa3 threatens Qa8+ and back rank mate.
Nf7# — the king is completely smothered by its own pieces.
Philidors Legacy — queen sacrifice into smothered mate.
Ne6 threatens Nf8# (smothered) and attacks g7.
Nf6+ interferes with queen-rook connection.
d5 interferes with coordination between knight c6 and bishop d7.
Capturing on d4 clears e3 and opens lines for the rook.
e6! sacrifices the pawn to clear e5 for the knight.
Creating an isolated queens pawn for Black. White exploits the weak d5 square.
c3 reinforces the d4-e5 pawn chain.
Bd3 completes development while eyeing h7.
cxd5 creates an open c-file and prepares e4.
e4! opens the center in QGD structures.
The knight lands on the powerful d5 outpost.
Kb2 occupies the key square for pawn promotion.
Ka1! is the only drawing move — stalemate after a1=Q.
Ke5 outflanks the black king.
Ka5 guards the a6 pawn.
Kd2 uses corresponding squares theory.
e6 with the opposition guarantees promotion.
Mutual zugzwang — whoever moves loses.
Ke5 wins the d5 pawn via opposition.
The Lucena bridge-building technique: Rf4 prepares to cut off the black king and create a bridge for the white king to emerge.
Build the bridge: Rf4-Re4-Ke7-Re1 allows the king to emerge safely.
The Philidor defensive technique: the rook on the 3rd rank prevents the enemy king from advancing.
The king must stay in front of the pawn to support its advance.
Active rook placement behind the passed pawn.
Kd4 takes the direct opposition, a fundamental endgame technique.
Ke4 seizes the opposition and the key squares.
White triangulates with Ka4-Ka3-Kb3 to lose a tempo and force Black into zugzwang.
A mutual zugzwang position. Being forced to move loses.
Qf5 creates an unstoppable mating net with Qg6/Qh7#.
Qe7 threatens Qf8# and Qxf7+.
Qf6 threatens Qg7# while the rook controls e8.
Ne7+ attracts the king, then Qb7+ wins the rook.
Nb5 threatens Nc7 forking rook and king.
